data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: Deeply Undervalued REIT Opportunity With Double-Digit Dividends
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Innovative Industrial Properties (NYSE:IIPR) is the only NYSE-listed cannabis REIT, with over 100 industrial properties across the US and 93% of their leases expiring after 2034. Due to dividend cut risks, major tenant defaults, and other macro headwinds, the stock is down about 60% over the past 12 months and over 80% from their 2021 peak, with very significant potential backed by long-term tailwinds, so let’s take a deeper look, as I believe this is a very impressive opportunity.

Despite overall excellent results in terms of AFFO per share (such as a 45% annual AFFO per diluted share CAGR from 2017 to 2024), the company has been facing significant pressure from tenants defaulting.

As we can see, the company’s AFFO took a significant hit, falling from $2.29 in Q2 2024 to $1.71 in Q2 2025 - a massive ~25.32% decline. Still, they are now working on reclaiming their properties and also replacing underperforming tenants, which is ultimately a key thing to remember about this kind of REIT. The fact is they own the properties, and other than some missed rent or maybe some investments in the defaulted tenants, they don’t stand to lose as much. This will, however, lead to near-term pressure on the stock, as we can see, although the company’s experienced management also mentioned during their Q2 Earnings Report:

(...) we are focused on optimizing occupancy across our portfolio to strengthen our tenant credit profiles and are actively pursuing all legal remedies available to enhance the performance of our real estate portfolio. I'd like to provide some additional color on our progress with each tenant.

Still, even an AFFO of $6.84 per share means about $193.69 million (vs. $256 million in 2024), which is still excellent for a $1.5 billion market cap, so even if it falls even more, the company still trades at a very solid ratio.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see yet another advantage, as Innovative Industrial Properties is one of the healthiest REITs we can find right now. Excluding the notes due in 2026 (5.5% rate), the company is virtually debt-free, while also having $99.67 million in cash and cash equivalents. Not only that, but there’s $1.88 billion in equity on a $1.5 billion market cap, meaning they are traded at a PB of ~0.8, which can offer a solid margin of safety especially when we think about their NAV that’s likely much higher than the equity.

Back in early October, they also announced that they secured a $100 million credit facility with a federally regulated commercial bank, which they plan to use as a way to fund part of their initial $105 million investment in IQHQ with a 14% expected combined yield, a life science real estate platform. I believe this deal shows that they aren’t afraid to steer the company into other directions if needed, and it’s also important to remember that if things go in an extremely bad direction for cannabis, the properties can be repurposed at the end of the day - at much worse rates probably, but they aren’t worthless.

The rest of the funding includes up to $165 million of preferred stock, which is expected to be funded in multiple tranches between Q4 2025 and the Q2 2027. This offers solid diversification in an area where the management actually has quite a lot of expertise, while they also pointed out that:

Those yields (...) would be significantly below our current cost of capital and investing in this way allowed us to have a very accretive transaction.

Innovative Industrial Properties’ Q3 Earnings Report is scheduled on November 3, with the Normalized FFO per share expected to reach $1.56 on a $63.72 million revenue. Given the multitude of internal and external factors that are advancing, I believe this report will definitely be worth paying attention to, as the company can offer a lot of insights into things such as how their expansion is going, potential tenant improvements, property reclaiming, macro developments, international expansion plans, etc., which can also lead to significant price moves.

Dividends & Buybacks

Their AFFO has previously been ranging between 80% and 90% since 2017, which is fine for a REIT, but we have to keep in mind the near-term impact of these bankruptcies. As their dividend grew together with the AFFO and the stock plunged significantly lately, we’re now looking at a yield that got even above 15% recently.

However, I would add that the dividend is now very questionable, as annualizing that $1.71 AFFO per share results in $6.84 per year, which is not enough to cover the $7.60 dividend. That’s important, especially since they’ll start issuing those preferred stocks for the IQHQ deal on top, and with the debt repayment due in 2026, I don’t think it’s impossible to see them scale it down a bit and argue that they need to focus on the business first before the dividend.

They don’t have to and can still support it for a year or two until they improve, but I believe they might choose to do it to not risk weakening the underlying business, and it’s likely something already priced in. It wouldn’t be a permanent thing, but I’d expect a cut just to improve their flexibility.

Assuming an 85% payout on that annualized $193.69 million, we’re at $164.63 million, meaning about 10.975% on the current price - which is still excellent, but a cut is generally seen as bad for the market even if it’s for the company’s own good.

Risks & Opportunities

Starting with some general opportunities, rate cuts in the US will likely be their best near-term catalyst. They would not only help IIRP through lowering the cost of current and future debt and fund their next stages of expansion, but would also help their tenants stabilize and expand again. The company ultimately depends on the activity of their tenants, and if the latter have issues, then IIPR would at least go through some slower times.

They also increase discretionary budgets, which can be quite solid for the company’s tenants, as they’d get both improving demand and better financing conditions, which is why this can trigger a recovery in the industry. Plus, I believe it’s actually great that the stock didn’t react that much to rate cuts back in September or when the market’s expectations for cuts improved (mostly due to the tenant issues pressuring the price), which is why I believe there is definitely “pent-up” potential in this industry.

Plus, we have to remember that IIPR pays a massive yield now after the stock fell so much. As a REIT that would likely improve on top, we could see treasury yields continue to fall thanks to rate cuts. Again, if we see treasuries falling from the recent 4-5% yields back to something like 2% over the longer-term, it would be very hard to see a REIT paying such a nice double-digit yield not move, as the opportunity cost and risk-adjusted return would make owning them much more attractive, as getting a very solid yield from the theoretically risk-free treasuries won’t be an option anymore.

Also, as we’ve seen with High Tide (HITI) recently, for example - key player in Canada’s cannabis market - the company doesn’t have to stick to one country. Germany or the Czech Republic, for example, are just a couple of new major options right now as legalization advances, and an expansion there could offer another decade of growth to a company like IIPR, at least. As HITI said after buying a majority interest in Remexian to enter the German market:

Remexian is already a leader in Germany, importing cannabis from around the world and distributing to hundreds of pharmacies and wholesalers. For the 6 months ended March 2025, the company generated annualized revenue of EUR 70 million and adjusted EBITDA of EUR 15 million, numbers that immediately add meaningful heft to our already-stellar business.

Just to point, pay attention to how much potential there is in this market, as EUR 70 million in revenue is seen as a market leader. And this is just one of the many potential other markets that can come with significant long-term growth as a bonus.

I’d also like to point out something I’ve said in the NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP) Strong Buy article posted recently. Unlike Medical Properties Trust (MPW) - which I’m assuming most people interested in REITs have at least briefly heard of - these two companies do get some more exposure to individual tenants due to the small property count (a bit over 100 here), but we have to keep in mind that these companies barely have any debt. In IIPR’s case, this exposure spreads better among their tenants, but the small number of properties still represents a higher point of risk, as even a small tenant going bankrupt can hurt their rent quite a bit.

What that means is that even if some tenants were to continue to go through hard times, we’re looking at some weaker years in terms of AFFOs and dividends while they find other tenants (as they are doing now), but they don’t have to sell their assets to pay some major upcoming debt, which is a massive difference. This means that IIPR and NLCP have significantly more financial flexibility and don’t have to rely on full capacity since every penny of rent they miss now means higher refinancing costs or a higher burden on tomorrow’s repayments - which has been the case for MPW.

As NLCP also points out, US legal cannabis sales are expected to grow at a CAGR of 7% (NewLake cites 7.2%) from 2024 to 2029, and several states are advancing towards becoming potential adult/medical-use markets. Especially as Innovative Industrial Properties is more than 5 times bigger than NewLake, I believe they are in a position to take advantage of this future growth even better since they’ve got much more capital available.

Again, we are at a point where the landscape in US cannabis legalization is characterized by rapid progress at the state level and also significant developments regarding federal policy, even as cannabis remains a federally controlled substance.

As NewLake also pointed out, we’ve seen some legal advancements, such as the recommendation to reschedule cannabis to Schedule III (from I) of the Controlled Substances Act, with NLCP saying that this change would actually alleviate the tax burden on its tenants, estimating that their tenants would collectively save over $400 million annually, which is expected to improve tenant credit quality and cash flows and spur incremental investment in the sector. And again, that’s on top of the beneficial effects of rate cuts, so we might be at a turnaround point.

As a note, Schedule III is significantly better than I for cannabis, being defined as moderate to low potential for physical/psychological dependence; accepted medical use compared to high potential for abuse, no accepted medical use, and lack of accepted safety, coming with far leaner regulatory pressure. This also comes with better tax relief, allowing companies to deduct ordinary expenses such as rent, payroll, etc., so definitely good for IIPR.

However, as this move would also get more banks in the mix, it can limit IIPR’s margins, as tenants won’t have to rely on them as much. But, overall, I believe it’s a net positive as it can lead to much more investing activity and tenants in the future, on top of improving the current tenants’ financial health and flexibility.

Still, even with all that looking like a clear trend and the increase in US acceptance of cannabis, that doesn’t mean all issues are over and the industry will simply recover overnight. This is still a very politicized subject, and there is definitely risk coming from the gap between state legalization and federal prohibition, systemic barriers (such as taxation, banking, research requirements), on top of the political uncertainty surrounding the US now.

There’s a reason why precious metals are where they are now and will probably have their best year in history in 2025. Not only do we see the industry’s own uncertainties, but they also have to deal with all the current fears around a trade war, political uncertainty, tariffs that can come overnight, rate cuts pressuring the US dollar even more, etc. So, even if IIPR has a solid financial position and makes good money even today, the management will have to continue navigating this well and not overleverage, for example.

Valuation

As for IIPR’s valuation, let’s go with that annualized AFFO of $193.69 million in 2025, which would grow to $250 million by 2028 (which represents a very slow recovery with no growth compared to 2024; well below their historical CAGR) just to be safer, after which it would grow at a 4% CAGR until year 10, with a 3% terminal growth rate as a result of the potential changes in the industry and international expansion in the long-term, with near-term catalysts like rate cuts and regulatory changes. I’ll assume a WACC of 10.5% (compared to my estimated 9.4% based on a cost of equity of ~10.2% and a ~5.6% cost of debt), as well as 28.317 million shares outstanding and $190.2 million in net debt as of Q2 (not including that extra credit facility for IQHQ until they give clear indicators for AFFO accretion). This would get us to an enterprise value of $3.1 billion and an equity value of $2.91 billion, meaning a price of $102.92 per share, which is nearly 2x from the current level for a relatively conservative estimate.

Conclusions

I rate Innovative Industrial Properties a Strong Buy even if I expect a dividend cut, as the company’s excellent financials, still strong AFFO generation, and long-term potential, plus the massive double-digit yield they can still afford to pay, are too hard to ignore. I placed a Buy order recently and will look forward to developing the position at lower prices. Risks remain, especially from a political point of view, but the company’s management seems competent and ready to continue navigating it in a good direction.

data.title: Lynas Rare Earths: Q1 Earnings Preview And Trump Trade Deal
data.description:
Lynas Rare Earths (OTCPK:LYSCF) is a rare earth miner located in Australia. I covered it in April, but after major gains in the stock and President Trump's recent deal with Australia over rare earths, I decided it was time to follow up and provide an updated thesis ahead of Q1 results.

I believe the gap between price and value has closed and have amended my rating to Hold accordingly.

Summary of Previous Thesis

I saw potential for Lynas to be a good cyclical play, particularly in the context of Trump's trade wars with China, where America sources 70% of its rare earths. This is a vulnerability Trump would be looking to shore up, but the tricky part is having the land, mines, and refineries in place to produce the minerals.

Lynas not only has the land; it has the assets in place to extract and process it. In fact, it had used the cyclical highs from three years ago to invest in the capex for the assets it has now. As the shares traded near $8 when revenues were higher, I believed there was similar upside here as well if Lynas could get more sales and better prices with American customers. This would provide significant free cash flow to support that price or potentially dividends, and so I rated the stock a Buy, while noting that Australia could also become a target in trade wars.

Financial Updates

As their fiscal years end in June, we have a more current annual report now than we had in April. Let's review some of the key details there.

For starters, sales were up, even though the cyclical upside has yet to be truly realized. Importantly, cost of sales increased even more, resulting in a smaller gross profit. Management explained:

Cost of sales increased due to higher fixed costs on lower production volumes at the new facilities which are being commissioned and ramped up.

This is important because it shows that investors cannot solely rely on upside from the top line. There can always be dangers to the bottom line and margins that shareholders ultimately get to eat.

The cash flow situation nevertheless showed an improvement. Operating cash flows rose from $35M AUD to $104M between 2024 and 2025. Additions to property & equipment showed significant cash outflows, but even this declined into 2025.

Looking at the balance sheet, the financial condition is healthy in spite of the heavy capex. They ended the period with $166.5M in cash, sufficient to cover about $152M in total debt.

Thus, in spite of the issue with gross margins, it was a good year for the company that has set it up well for fiscal 2026.

Outlook and Valuation

First, let's consider the deal that Trump struck with Australia, and I'll cite the PBS report that I linked earlier. It entails $8.6B USD of investment between the two countries to build up rare earth mining capacity, particularly in Australia. Trump was quoted as saying:

In about a year from now we’ll have so much critical mineral and rare earth that you won’t know what to do with them. They’ll be worth $2.

The report also explained how China's capacity took decades of work to develop and that matching it, even on an expedited schedule, will still take many years. Investors can get two general ideas from this:

  • Additional capex fund supported by subsidies
  • Long-term easing of supply situation

If we look at the capex guidance from the annual report, it was about a third of fiscal 2025's. Even $100M AUD of it was to close out existing projects in its Malaysian assets. Nevertheless, there is reason to think that substantially more capex could occur down the road.

For one, they have guided for investments for eventual operations in the United States. Lynas is positioned to have its operations subsidized by both countries, while generating free cash flow. This can reduce the risk of these capital investments for shareholders over time, but that doesn't make them disappear entirely.

As Austro-American cooperation is long-term in its impact, only a mismatch in rare earth supply and demand could be a source of additional cash flow in the near term, and that will depend on how well Trump brokers a deal with China.

Revenue history shows how high the top line can get when supply is constrained, as was the case in 2022 when the Russian invasion of Ukraine disrupted global supply chains, which were still recovering from the impacts of COVID.

With the increased capacity from their Malaysian assets, they can at least double their output, compared to prior years. Per the annual report, both Australian and Malaysian assets can do about 10,500 REO tonnes per annum. Doubled productivity could rationally support $200M AUD of operating cash flows, possibly higher if prices favor them.

Yet, there is reason to think that LYSCF is fairly valued for its cash flows. For one, Lynas took advantage of the stock price increase since April and sold more shares for $750M AUD, at $13.25 per share. This was only about 6% of the equity, but as they aren't guiding for dividends or buybacks either, I think it helps us connect some dots between intrinsic value and capital allocation.

In USD, Lynas's market cap is about $12B. Even if production and demand could push annual free cash flow to $500M AUD, that would only work out to $325M USD at current rates. Given the $750M capital raise as well, it seems unlikely that shareholders will be winning any cash flow for some time.

Even if we believe rare earths are what's hot right now, a capital-intensive business like Lynas has a multiple of 36 for that very favorable and fortunate hypothetical of free cash flow. Recall the chart I shared, which showed that the average selling price per REO tonne at the peak of 2022 was 19% higher than 2025's. It wasn't two or three times as high.

If we think academically, I can see how a portfolio manager might buy LYSCF as some sort of hedge against a "China rare earths risk," but I'm an investor, not an academic. The relationship between price and upside just doesn't seem to be what it was anymore.

I would want to see an entry price similar to what we had in April, under $6 per share, before considering this undervalued again.

As fiscal Q1 results approach at the end of October, we will want to assess revenue, production, price per REO tonne, and any amendments to capex that surely follow the capital raise.

Conclusion

I wasn't expecting Lynas to deliver its upside in a few months, but when it happens, one needs to reassess. I think LYSCF's gains reflect a catching up of market belief with the opportunity back in April, and I do not believe that opportunity is vastly different than before. Because of that, I downgrade the stock to Hold until a safe entry price is available again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Deckers: Q2 Sell-Off Created A Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK) reported the company’s fiscal Q2 results from the July-September period on the 23rd of October. The footwear company continued to show strength in the two primary brands, HOKA and UGG, but a weaker-than-expected FY2026 sales guidance has sent the stock down -11% in pre-market action at the time of writing.

I cautiously remained at a Hold rating in my previous July article, titled “Deckers: An Attractive Entry Point Is Nearing”. The stock has since lost -14% of its value, continuing to underperform the S&P 500's 8% return. I believe that an attractive entry point is finally here, as the market has become overly concerned.

Deckers’ Q2 Financials Were Good

Despite the very negative post-earnings market reaction, Deckers’ surface-level fiscal Q2 results were consistent and even exceeded expectations, especially in terms of profitability.

Revenues grew by 9.1% to $1.43 billion, very slightly beating Deckers’ own $1.38-1.42 billion guidance range given for the quarter. The topline result also beat Wall Street’s consensus, modeled after Deckers’ own expectations, by $13 million. The two primary brands, HOKA and UGG, both showed continued momentum with 11.1% and 10.1% year-on-year growth, respectively. Domestic sales declined by -1.7%, but strong 29.3% international growth drove growth. Deckers notes that the HOKA brand has continued to grow in awareness globally, driving growth. Overall, sales momentum has remained fairly strong despite a slower growth rate than in previous years.

The EPS came in at $1.82, beating the given $1.5-1.55 guidance range by a very clear margin. The EPS also beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.24 as a result. Behind the beat, Deckers still managed to mitigate tariffs’ impact on margins by bringing additional inventory ahead of tariffs – the gross margin even improved by 30 basis points to 56.2% despite the U.S.’s tariffs on Vietnam and China. Deckers’ operating income grew by 7.0% to $326.5 million, still reflecting some margin pressure from growth in SG&A expenses as Deckers is investing heavily into marketing. The good earnings performance underlines Deckers’ strong Q2 on the surface.

Ultimately, the significant Q2 EPS beat isn’t cause for much celebration – Deckers still expects tariffs to hit margins in the second half of FY2026 as tariffed inventory starts to be sold. The FY2026 gross margin guidance is set at 56%, reflecting significant pressure in H2 after Deckers achieved a 57.9% gross margin in FY2025. The FY2026 net tariff impact is expected to be $55-75 million after mitigation, only reflecting around half a year's worth of tariffs. Tariff mitigation efforts are still largely ongoing.

Brand Momentum Concerns Are Increasing, But I Don’t Think the Growth Story Is Over Yet

Rather than Q2 financials, the market’s concerns are based on other signals of weakness. Surface-level revenue growth has remained good, but the future growth rate is under increasing scrutiny.

As the most notable concern, Deckers provided a full-year revenue guidance with the Q2 report. The FY2026 topline is expected at $5.35 billion, suggesting 7.3% growth for the fiscal year with low-teens growth for HOKA and low-to-mid single-digit growth for UGG. The guidance implies only 3.7% growth for the second half – Deckers’ growth seems to be decelerating quite rapidly. Wall Street expected much better $5.5 billion revenues for the year prior to the report, causing the weak guidance to send concerning signals to the market.

There are also some subtle signals in the Q2 report, suggesting that Deckers’ brand momentum is slowing. Second quarter growth was achieved in part due to earlier wholesale shipment timing, and direct-to-consumer revenues that better reflect short-term consumer demand even declined by -0.8%. HOKA still showed 8% growth in DTC sales driven by international demand, but UGG’s DTC sales plummeted by -10%. Management relates UGG’s sharp DTC decline to better in-stock levels with wholesale partners, shifting consumer demand to multi-brand retailers. The U.S. marketplace has been increasingly challenging for Deckers with the HOKA brand as well, being the clearly most important market at 59% of Q2 revenues. Bernstein already noted in September that Deckers’ brands are running out of steam.

Past only the slow FY2026 sales guidance and DTC weakness, where are Deckers’ main brands in terms of brand relevance? Google Trends suggests a mixed performance between brands. HOKA has continued to show strong year-on-year gains in search volume, but UGG’s relevance has started to lag from September forward. Especially HOKA is improving brand recognition internationally, gradually moving importance from the U.S. into other markets as well. While guided second-half revenues suggest significant deceleration after Deckers’ very strong brand run in FY2021-FY2025, I don’t believe that the long-term growth story is over yet, as HOKA still clearly has room to run. Deckers’ Q2 report marks the 16th consecutive revenue consensus beat as the company seems to guide conservatively; the $5.35 billion FY2026 revenue guidance is also likely guided cautiously.

An Attractive Entry Point Is Finally Here

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Deckers. I slightly lowered my revenue estimates, now reflecting a FY2025-FY2035 CAGR of 5.1% through HOKA’s growth. I assume Deckers to slightly beat its FY2026 revenue guidance. I have kept the perpetual growth estimate at 2.5%.

Deckers has maintained high margins well in an uncertain tariff environment, and the 21.5% operating margin guidance for FY2026 suggests that margins will remain good. Through additional growth and tariff mitigation, I assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at the 21.5% level from FY2027 forward. Deckers’ cash flow should continue to follow after-tax earnings well.

The estimates put Deckers’ fair value at $126.7, 39% above the stock price at the time of writing. I believe that an attractive entry point is finally here, as the market has become overly concerned with Deckers’ decelerating growth. The brand momentum risk is real, but the valuation provides a strong risk-to-reward.

The DCF model would estimate 11% upside to $100.7 even with 2% growth from FY2027 forward. The market currently prices in earnings contraction, an expectation that is not supported by current momentum.

The fair value estimate is down slightly from $131.6 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.45%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don’t assume Deckers to draw debt. For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.00% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.93. With a liquidity premium of 0.15%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 8.45%.

Takeaway

Deckers’ Q2 sent the stock crashing. While revenue momentum was consistent with expectations and the EPS showed a clear beat, the market is increasingly concerned with Deckers’ brand momentum. The FY2026 revenue guidance was set low, and Deckers’ DTC growth has been increasingly weak. I believe that the concern is now overpriced in Deckers’ valuation – HOKA still has a strong long-term runway, especially internationally, and the growth story isn’t over yet despite clear deceleration. I upgrade my rating on DECK stock to Buy based on good upside in the valuation.

data.title: First Citizens: A Pullback Worth Buying (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of First Citizens BancShares (NASDAQ:FCNCA) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 14% of their value. Indeed, within the past few weeks, shares approached their April lows even as the market has surged to record highs, reflecting the immense fear around private credit losses. With its Silicon Valley Bank unit, FCNCA has higher perceived exposure to capital markets volatility and nontraditional lending, bringing these pressures into focus. I last covered shares in August, rating First Citizens a “hold,” though with shares down 5% while the market has gained 5%, arguably a “sell” rating was justified. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit FCNCA with shares at the $1,750 level that I described as a “good entry point.”

’In the company’s third quarter, First Citizens earned $44.62, which beat estimates by $3.13. The bank’s aggressive share repurchase program has helped to boost earnings by reducing share count, and we are starting to see some acceleration in balance sheet growth, which is encouraging. That said, there were $82 million charge-off tied to First Brands, a failure that has caused losses across much of the industry. On the earnings call, management did say that this loss “is not reflective of broader issues” in its loan book, and its $300 million supply chain finance business is small, limiting potential losses even if this commentary proves overly optimistic.

Drilling into results, First Citizens reported an adjusted NIM of 3.15%, up 1 bp sequentially. This was still down 18 bps from a year ago, as Fed rate cuts have reduced margins, given the bank has a smaller fixed rate reinvestment tailwind than peers. While it lacks this tailwind, it also has a much smaller unrealized loss on securities than most regional banks, which has allowed it to more aggressively return capital to shareholders. Given balance sheet growth, net interest income grew by $44 million sequentially, but it is still lower than last year by about 1%.

On its balance sheet, the bank ended the quarter with $163 billion in deposits, up $2 billion from Q2 and 7% from last year. So far this cutting cycle, FCNCA has passed on 39% of rate cuts to deposits, which is a bit slower than the 47% upside beta they had in the hiking cycle. Towards the latter part of the hiking cycle, banks had to more willingly pass on rate increases as deposit balances came under strain, and it has been difficult to reverse rates as aggressively. This is one reason why margins have come under pressure. Still, First Citizens’ beta is consistent with the 35-45% that I am broadly expecting the industry to produce. Importantly, it does appear that noninterest-bearing (“NIB”) balances have clearly bottomed, a positive for margins going forward.

On the other side of its balance sheet, First Citizens has $145 billion in loans, up $3.5 billion sequentially and 4% from last year. SVB global fund loans grew by 10% sequentially, driving sequential loan growth. SVB accounted for nearly all of the company’s loan growth as traditional C&I and real estate demand both remain low. Given muted borrower demand in the elevated rate environment, it has been difficult for FCNCA to grow its loan book as quickly as it has its deposit base, which has left the balance sheet with excess liquidity and pressured margins.

It has $36 billion of commercial real estate, accounting for about 27% of its portfolio, slightly below norms for regional banks. We are also seeing increased borrowing from its technology customers in the SVB unit. About half of this business is to software customers, and the VC environment does remain very strong, which should continue to support credit quality.

Still, we are seeing definite pressure in credit quality. Its 65 bps of charge-offs were up from the ~40 bp range over the past year. This was largely due to the First Brands loss, accounting for over a third of its $234 million of net charge-offs. During the quarter, it set aside $191 million for credit losses, up by $76 million, primarily due to higher charge-offs. Total reserves were down by $20 million to $1.65 billion.

It now has reserves that cover 1.14% of loans, down 7 bps from a year ago. Even as it has allowed reserves to come down, its nonaccrual loans continue to move higher. They now stand at $1.4 billion, up ~$90 million sequentially and ~$160 million from last year.

As a result, it has just 117% coverage of NPLs, down from 134% last year. This is a very low level, and this decline in reserve coverage is a concern for me, given the economic outlook remains highly uncertain and credit quality is not showing signs of improvement. Even with a largely secured loan book, I like to see banks carry at least 150% coverage, especially given recent flare-ups in credit markets. This suggests that FCNA should carry another ~$500 million of reserves. As such, I do not expect further reserve decreases, and in the event of a recession, we would likely see renewed reserve builds. Given its excess capital position, FCNCA can manage through this period with somewhat lower reserves, but I view this as a negative offset to capital.

Looking to its capital position, First Citizens has an 11.65% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio. Given buybacks, capital is down 50 bps sequentially and about 1.6% from last year. FCNCA was significantly overcapitalized after integrating SVB, and it has been returning this excess capital via buybacks to normalize its capital posture. As I have written previously, I view ~10.5-11% as a strong place for FCNCA to operate, meaning it can continue to return excess capital via buybacks to investors.

Indeed, the company did $900 million of share repurchases during the quarter. Over the past 5 quarters, it has bought back about 15% of its stock, and its share count is down nearly 11% over the past year. While it still has some excess capital, the amount has declined, and as a result, I do expect the pace of share count reduction to slow going forward, though it should still be at least 6-8% over the next year, in my view. FCNCA has just a 0.5% dividend yield, so buybacks are the focus of capital returns.

One other reason why buybacks may slow is that FCNCA is putting some capital into inorganic expansion. Last week, First Citizens announced it would be acquiring 138 branches from the Bank of Montreal (BMO), primarily across the central US. This acquisition will give FCNCA about $5.7 billion of new deposits and $1.1 billion in loans. FCNCA will pay a net deposit premium of about 5%, or just under $300 million.

With a ~3% NIM, this acquisition has a sub-2 year payback. I view this as a very attractive payback and likely to be more accretive over a 3-5 year time window than buybacks. Given its small size, I do not see integration or antitrust review risk. It expects to close this deal in the middle of 2026. It will boost FCNCA’s deposit base by 3%, making this a manageable, bolt-on acquisition. It has no specific M&A plans beyond BMO, but management noted that M&A is a key pillar of its long-term growth strategy.

Management expects deposits to be broadly flat in Q4 vs Q3 while loans should stay in the $143-$146 billion area—I see some upside here. Given the First Brands loss, it now expects about 47bps of charge-offs this year. Assuming two rate cuts, management expects NIM and NII to bottom in Q1 2026, which is consistent with my view.

Back in August, I saw FCNCA having about $160-$170 in underlying earnings power with another $170 in excess capital, driving a fair value of ~$1900. With another quarter of buybacks and financials, I see FCNCA having ~$167-$177 of go-forward earnings and about $100 of excess capital, net against its lighter reserves. At ~11x earnings reflecting its margin bottom, I see about $1960 as fair value. This gives shares about 11% upside.

As such, I am upgrading shares to a “buy.” With ongoing buybacks, a solid balance sheet, and improving deposit performance, FCNCA is now an attractive risk/reward opportunity at less than 1.1x its $1630 tangible book value. I view this underperformance as having created an attractive entry point.
